intro: The biggest real distinction between Appwrite and Convex is straightforward: Appwrite carries low lock-in while Convex has high lock-in. Appwrite sits at hybrid pricing with $25/mo/project (Pro), a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries low lock-in, while Convex sits at hybrid pricing with $25/member/mo (Pro), a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries high lock-in. Appwrite is a fit when you need mobile, teams — typical scenarios include mvp/prototyping speed. Convex is aimed at mobile, teams, with workloads like mvp/prototyping speed as the common case. The honest trade-off: Appwrite is explicitly not for complex custom business logic; Convex is not intended for complex custom business logic. Lock-in is the factor most teams underestimate: Appwrite and Convex have different exit paths, and that shapes total cost of ownership over a two-to-three year horizon. Before committing, audit where each tool stores state, which APIs you depend on, and how painful a switch would be six months in.
quick_take: Appwrite is for mobile, teams; Convex is for mobile, teams; decide based on how much lock-in you can accept.
verdict_a: Choose Appwrite if your primary workload is mobile, teams and hybrid pricing ($25/mo/project (Pro)) is acceptable.
verdict_b: Choose Convex if your primary workload is mobile, teams and hybrid pricing ($25/member/mo (Pro)) is acceptable.

faq.a: It depends on workload shape: Appwrite lists $25/mo/project (Pro), while Convex lists $25/member/mo (Pro). With different pricing models (hybrid vs hybrid), the cheaper option flips based on seat count, usage volume, and whether you use the free tier. Do a per-workload estimate rather than comparing headline numbers.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Appwrite to Convex?
faq.a: Migrating from Appwrite to Convex is generally feasible because Appwrite has low lock-in, so data and configuration are usually portable. Since both tools sit in the same category (Backend-as-a-Service), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep Appwrite running in parallel until Convex is verified.

Our data gives Appwrite and Convex the same developer experience score (5/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Convex a good alternative to Appwrite?
faq.a: Convex is in the same category as Appwrite (Backend-as-a-Service), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Both tools claim similar best-fit workloads on paper, so the real test is how each behaves under your specific constraints. Note that Convex is explicitly not for complex custom business logic — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
